mergeRationale: The action validates only the path and rebuilds the server-side fetch URL from the client-controllable Host header, so a forged Host turns it into a server-side request to an attacker-chosen origin (SSRF).

requiredFindings.summary: The patch checks the path but builds the server-side fetch URL from the request Host header.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: host and x-forwarded-proto are request headers controllable by a client or intermediary; a forged Host (e.g. 127.0.0.1:8080 or an external host) makes the server fetch an internal or attacker-controlled origin. Relative-path safety and origin trust are two separate problems.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Build the URL from a trusted canonical origin (config), or enforce a host allowlist, before any server-side fetch.

requiredFindings.summary: The tests do not cover attacker-controlled Host or X-Forwarded-Host.
requiredFindings.expectedReasoning: The added test only asserts a relative path works; the absolute-URL rejection test also passes while the SSRF path (forged host) stays exploitable and untested.
requiredFindings.acceptableFix: Add tests for forged Host, forwarded host, scheme changes, and private-IP origins.

disallowedConclusions: The patch is safe because it only allows relative paths.
Trusting the Host header is fine because the app is behind a proxy.
